notable.notable_blurb: Co-discovered Vitamin A, 1913; founded amino acid science

career.notes: Spent entire career here. Researches on vegetable proteins beginning 1888. Published 'The Vegetable Proteins' (1909). With Lafayette Mendel co-discovered Vitamin A in 1913, established essential amino acids (lysine and tryptophan) in healthy diet.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale science PhDs through different Blake siblings
cross_connections.notes: HD4112 (Yale PhD chemistry 1885) and HD4101 (Yale PhD math/physics 1925) — both descend from Eli Whitney Blake (X00503) and Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake (HD1556) through different children. The Blake-Whitney inventive tradition expressed through two generations of Yale scientists.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-discovered Vitamin A; founded amino acid science
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Burr Osborne and Lafayette Mendel — colleagues at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station — independently co-discovered Vitamin A in 1913, the same year that Elmer McCollum and Marguerite Davis published the same finding three weeks earlier. Both papers appeared in the same issue of the Journal of Biological Chemistry; both teams established that vitamin deficiency caused xerophthalmia, opening modern nutritional biochemistry.

narrative_blocks.header: Isolated essential amino acids in rat nutrition studies
narrative_blocks.body: Working at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station with 200 carefully fed rats, Osborne and Mendel showed which amino acids — lysine and tryptophan among them — are essential in a healthy diet. Casein became the first identified 'complete protein,' establishing nutrition science beyond caloric energy and toward structural amino acid requirements.

Osborne was elected to the United States National Academy of Sciences in 1910, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1914, and the American Philosophical Society in 1921 — the three foundational American scientific societies. He published over a hundred papers, most jointly with his collaborator Lafayette Mendel.

Francis died in 1900 at the age of two.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Arthur Osborne
married his son, the lawyer Arthur Dimon Osborne

narrative_blocks.header: He sat in Congress as a Connecticut Whig
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Burr Osborne represented Connecticut in the United States House as a Whig from 1839 to 1843. The family name would pass to a grandson, the chemist who helped discover vitamin A.
His grandson and namesake Thomas Burr Osborne, the Yale protein chemist, co-discovered Vitamin A. The congressman’s own exact dates and his wife’s identity remain under research.

orbit_connections.name: Gov. Henry Baldwin Harrison (via Frances Louisa Blake Osborn)
orbit_connections.relationship: Arthur Dimon Osborn — son of US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne, and husband of HD1572 Frances Louisa Blake — had a sister Elizabeth who married Governor Henry Baldwin Harrison of Connecticut
orbit_connections.notes: Frances Louisa Blake (HD1572) married Arthur Dimon Osborn. Arthur's father was US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne (CT). Arthur's sister Elizabeth married Henry Baldwin Harrison, Governor of Connecticut 1885-1887. Their son Thomas Burr Osborn (chemist) and Arthur Sherwood Osborn were grandchildren of Eli Whitney Blake. (Wikipedia on Eli Whitney Blake)

notable.notable_blurb: Blake stone crusher inventor, nephew of Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Patented the Blake stone crusher for road-building
narrative_blocks.detail: Blake graduated Yale in 1816 and trained at Litchfield Law School before abandoning law to work at his uncle Eli Whitney's armory in Whitneyville. He also held the first US mortise lock patent (1833, Blake Brothers) and served as president of the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences. His son Henry Taylor Blake (HD1568) went on to build New Haven's park system — the stone-crusher's roads and the park commissioner's parks both serving the same expanding American city. (Wikipedia; research_notes)
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Whitney Blake patented his stone crusher in 1857. The first commercially successful machine of its kind, it made macadam roads practical on a national scale and is credited as foundational to American infrastructure. He is in the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

narrative_blocks.header: He left law for his uncle’s armory
narrative_blocks.body: Litchfield-trained, Blake abandoned the law for Eli Whitney’s armory; his 1833 mortise lock was the first such American patent, and his 1857 stone crusher put him in the National Inventors Hall of Fame. He died at ninety-one.

documents.title: 1830 letter from George Washington Blake (Boston) to Elihu Blake Jr. (NYC) mentioning Eli Whitney Blake
documents.description: Stampless letter from G.W. Blake (1803-?, brother) in Boston to Elihu Blake Jr. (1793-1875, brother) at No. 5 Park Place, NYC. Mentions Eli Whitney Blake by name in connection with Mr. W. prescribing 'be married before the first day of next August' for his malady. Also mentions parents Pa (Elihu Blake Sr.) enjoys tolerable health and mother (Elizabeth Fay Whitney) is well; siblings Maria, Elizabeth, Frances, Josiah Whitney, John Adams; uncle Blake; Aunt Whitney in New Haven; the September 1830 boiler explosion on the steamboat United States. Authors not built in JSON per Jalapeño Rule but documented here for family-correspondence record.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law there before abandoning law for the Whitney Armory.

career.organization: Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences

cross_connections.display_label: was her mother-in-law; Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two children to disease in early 1836
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1836 Eliza Maria buried two of her young children within eight weeks — six-year-old Robert Pierrepont (HD1569) on January 17 and two-year-old Eliza Maria (HD1571) on March 13. She gave the name Eliza Maria again to a daughter born five years later (HD1576), passing down the name from the daughter she had lost.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST144.name: American Philosophical Society
INST144.notes:
Founded 1743 Philadelphia by Benjamin Franklin. The oldest learned society in the United States, modeled on the Royal Society of London. Membership election is one of the highest academic honors in American intellectual life — typically limited to roughly 1,000 living members worldwide.

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 7 elected members across the colonial and 19th-century period:

EARLY REPUBLIC ERA:
- HD0346 President Ezra Stiles (1727-1795, Yale president, elected during early APS expansion)
- T01807 Captain Andrew Talcott (1797-1883, military engineer, Mexican War + Mississippi River surveys)

ANTEBELLUM ERA:
- TD0239 Judge Joel Jones (1795-1860, elected 1848 documented — Philadelphia jurist + Mayor of Philadelphia 1849-1851)

VICTORIAN ERA YALE CLUSTER:
- HD1973 Theodore Dwight Woolsey (1801-1889, Yale president 1846-1871)
- X00898 Edward Salisbury Dana (1849-1935, Yale mineralogist)
- HD4112 Thomas Burr Osborne (1859-1929, Yale agricultural chemist — discovered proteins in seeds)

GILDED AGE:
- HD1687 Merrill Edwards Gates (1848-1922, Rutgers + Amherst president)

Joel Jones's 1848 election as APS member coincided with his service as Mayor of Philadelphia — putting him at the center of the city's mid-19th-century intellectual life alongside the broader Philadelphia learned-society world (Franklin Institute, Athenaeum, etc.).
